step2_rag_chunks: Reward yourself for milestones such as staying smoke-free for a day, a week, or a month.
Stress is a common trigger for smoking. Relaxation techniques such as deep breathing, meditation, or short walks can help manage stress without using tobacco.

Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.

step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? - If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.

- It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.
[Recent Conversation]
Therapist: The patient is saying that they think walking to work could help them clear their head and get their thoughts together before starting work, which sounds like a beneficial effect.
Can you imagine how it might feel to start your day feeling more focused and prepared, and how that might impact your ability to deal with stress and pressure?

Patient: Yeah... it sounds nice, I guess. Like, if I'm feeling more put together, I might not be as tempted to smoke when things get crazy. And maybe I'll be able to handle stressful meetings better, without falling back into old habits. But, I don't know... it's still just a feeling, and it's easy to say that it'll happen, but it's hard to actually make it happen.
Therapist: The patient is expressing uncertainty and skepticism about their ability to make a change, feeling like it's just a feeling and not a reality.
What makes you think that this is just a feeling, rather than something you can actually make happen?

Patient: I guess I just feel like I've been down this road before, and it's easy to get all excited about the idea of quitting, but it's hard to actually stick to it. Like, I said it worked for 3 months last time, but that was just a fluke, right? This time will be the same, and I'll just end up falling back into my old habits.
Therapist: The patient is saying that they feel like they've tried quitting before and it didn't work, so they're skeptical that they can actually make a change this time.

Is that skepticism stemming from the fact that you felt like you were really making progress the first time you quit for 3 months, and now you're worried that you won't be able to replicate that success?

Patient: I guess that's part of it. But I think what's really bothering me is that I feel like I let myself down, you know? I proved to myself that I could quit, and then I went and failed again. It's hard to live with that, and it makes me feel like maybe I'm just not cut out for this.

step2_rag_chunks: Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.
If you slip and smoke a cigarette, it does not mean you have failed. Many people attempt quitting several times before succeeding.
Reward yourself for milestones such as staying smoke-free for a day, a week, or a month.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: The patient has signaled they are ready to act or is wrapping up. Your ONLY job is to give ONE warm closing sentence. Do NOT ask any question. Do NOT suggest anything new. Do NOT summarize what was discussed. Just acknowledge their readiness warmly and wish them well. Example: 'That sounds like a solid plan — take it one step at a time.' One sentence only. Nothing more.
